items.q: What property do shape memory alloys have?
items.correct: They return to a pre-set shape when heated.
items.misconception: They remember every shape they have ever been bent into.
items.why: Students think SMAs are infinitely programmable. They actually have ONE 'remembered' shape, set during high-temperature treatment. Heating restores that shape; the alloy doesn't store a history of deformations.

items.q: What gives carbon fibre composite its strength?
items.correct: The fibres provide tensile strength; the resin matrix holds them in place and transfers loads.
items.misconception: The carbon fibres alone - the resin is just glue.
items.why: Students dismiss the matrix as filler. In reality the matrix is essential: it transfers loads between fibres and protects them. Loose carbon fibres without matrix have very little structural strength.

items.q: What is the difference between ductility and malleability?
items.correct: Ductility is being drawn into wires; malleability is being hammered into sheets.
items.misconception: They mean exactly the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Ductility is for metals, malleability is for plastics.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms because both involve deformation. Ductility = tensile (stretching into wires); malleability = compressive (rolling/hammering into sheets). Some metals excel at one but not the other.
The answer examiners want is: 2004. “1994” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Carbon nanotubes are about how many times stronger than steel by tensile strength?
items.why: It's 50x. “5x” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a cutting tool has hardness 1500 HV and tool steel has 800 HV, what is the ratio (rounded to 1 dp)?
items.why: Exact answer: 1.9. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
